Off Calendar Feb-18-2026 Text Ruling

Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production, Set One
Off Calendar Oct-08-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiffs' motion to compel further responses to their requests for production, set one, is granted.

Plaintiffs allege that defendant's defective (a) fare gates and (b) platform design and employee negligence were a substantial factor in causing their decedent's death. Plaintiffs are entitled to discovery that "appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." (CCP 2017.010.)

Plaintiffs' initial requests 4, 6, 7, 8, 11-15, 25-26, 30, and 42, which were not limited in time and pertained to all BART stations, were overbroad. Plaintiffs subsequently agreed to limit these requests to five years (1/1/19 to 12/31/24) and eight stations (Powell Street BART Station; West Oakland; 12th St. Oakland City Center; 19th St. Oakland; MacArthur; 24th St. Mission; Civic Center/ UN Plaza; and Coliseum Station.) Plaintiffs also proposed refinements of certain definitions to limit the scope of discovery. (Montiel Decl., Ex. E [7/8/25 letter].)

The court concludes that the requests at issue, as modified by Plaintiffs in informal resolution efforts, seek discoverable information. In Morlin v. State of California (1993) 12 Cal.App.4th 812, 817, the court held that incidents at other DMV locations were discoverable where the other locations had similar layouts. The same is true here. Defendant fails to show that the BART platforms or gates are markedly different from station to station. For example, there is no argument that some platforms have barriers while others do not. "Their relevance would be based on the fact that the state knew or should have known of the unusual risk associated with DMV parking lots." (Id.) Here, too, incidents at other stations with the same fare gates and lack of barriers as the station where Plaintiffs' decedent was killed are potentially admissible or reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence.

The court orders defendant to produce responsive documents to the RFPs at issue within 15 days of notice of entry of the order. If discovery is voluminous, defendant may make a rolling production but shall complete production within 60 days of notice of entry of the order.

Neither party seeks sanctions. The court concludes in any event that both parties' positions were substantially justified.

Proposed order submitted.

MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. SARVENAZ FAHIMI, Esq; Atty. VASTI MONTIEL, Esq.; Atty. NANCI NISHIMURA, Esq. and Atty. GIA JUNG, Esq. of Cotchett, Pitre & McCarthy (tel: 650-697-6000) for the Plaintiffs.
Atty. PETER VAN ZANDT, Esq. and Atty. NICHOLAS SYREN, Esq. of Allen, Glaessner, Hazelwood & Werth, LLP (tel: 415-697-2000) for the Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO BAY AREA RAPID TRANSIT DISTRICT's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District's ("BART") motion to strike is granted in part and denied in part.

Per CCP 436(a), the court may "[s]trike out any irrelevant, false, or improper matter inserted in any pleading." BART contends that several allegations in the complaint are improper:

Common carrier allegations (Paragraph 98): The motion is granted without leave to amend. "While this heightened standard applies to a passenger in transit, it generally does not apply to a passenger waiting in, or passing though, a station or terminal." (Churchman v. Bay Area Rapid Transit Dist. (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 246 [BART did not owe common carrier duty to patron injured on BART platform]; McGettigan v. Bay Area Rapid Transit District (1997) 57 Cal.App.4th 1011 [same].) The court is persuaded by the recent case of Smith v. Magic Mountain LLC (2024) 106 Cal.App.5th 1128, a platform case where the court articulated three elements necessary to find common carrier duties applicable in windows of time before and after transit: "(1) the person demonstrates intent to become a passenger, (2) the carrier takes some action indicating acceptance of the person as a passenger or does something by way of an invitation to the person to board, and (3) the person is placed under the control of the carrier." (Id. at 1138-1139 [internal quotation marks, citations, and brackets omitted].) Here, Ms. Dandan was not under control of the carrier as articulated in Smith because she was able to exit the BART platform and bypass transit at the time she was pushed. (Id. at p. 1132.) It does not appear to the court that Plaintiffs can plead in a manner that can satisfy the third element articulated in Smith.

Employee negligence allegations (Paragraphs 96-97): The motion is denied. Plaintiffs are only obliged to plead ultimate facts. The adduced paragraphs are sufficiently pleaded and BART's desire for more detail is a matter for discovery.

Claim for punitive damages, treble damages, and attorneys fees under the Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Protection Act, Welfare and Institutions Code section 15600, et seq. (Paragraphs 130-133): The motion is denied as moot since the elder abuse claim is not viable, as discussed in the court's companion order.

Order submitted.
